Therefore, when\n    /// we convert the principal trait-ref into a normal trait-ref,\n    /// you must give *some* self type. A common choice is `mk_err()`\n    /// or some placeholder type.\n    pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ty::Binder<I, TraitRef<I>> {\n        self.map_bound(|trait_ref| trait_ref.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty))\n    }\n}\n\n/// A `ProjectionPredicate` for an `ExistentialTraitRef`.\n#[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\n#[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub struct ExistentialProjection<I: Interner> {\n    pub def_id: I::DefId,\n    pub args: I::GenericArgs,\n    pub term: I::Term,\n\n    /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `ExistentialProjection`\n    /// without using [`ExistentialProjection::new_from_args`].\n    use_existential_projection_new_instead: (),\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for ExistentialProjection<I> {}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> ExistentialProjection<I> {\n    pub fn new_from_args(\n        interner: I,\n        def_id: I::DefId,\n        args: I::GenericArgs,\n        term: I::Term,\n    ) -> ExistentialProjection<I> {\n        interner.debug_assert_existential_args_compatible(def_id, args);\n        Self { def_id, args, term, use_existential_projection_new_instead: () }\n    }\n\n    pub fn new(\n        interner: I,\n        def_id: I::DefId,\n        args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\n        term: I::Term,\n    ) -> ExistentialProjection<I> {\n        let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\n        Self::new_from_args(interner, def_id, args, term)\n    }\n\n    /// Extracts the underlying existential trait reference from this projection.\n    /// For example, if this is a projection of `exists T. <T as Iterator>::Item == X`,\n    /// then this function would return an `exists T. T: Iterator` existential trait\n    /// reference.\n    pub fn trait_ref(&self, interner: I) -> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\n        let def_id = interner.parent(self.def_id);\n        let args_count = interner.generics_of(def_id).count() - 1;\n        let args = interner.mk_args(&self.args.as_slice()[..args_count]);\n        ExistentialTraitRef::new_from_args(interner, def_id.try_into().unwrap(), args)\n    }\n\n    pub fn with_self_ty(&self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\n        // otherwise the escaping regions would be captured by the binders\n        debug_assert!(!self_ty.has_escaping_bound_vars());\n\n        ProjectionPredicate {\n            projection_term: AliasTerm::new(\n                interner,\n                self.def_id,\n                [self_ty.into()].iter().chain(self.args.iter()),\n            ),\n            term: self.term,\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn erase_self_ty(interner: I, projection_predicate: ProjectionPredicate<I>) -> Self {\n        // Assert there is a Self.\n        projection_predicate.projection_term.args.type_at(0);\n\n        Self {\n            def_id: projection_predicate.projection_term.def_id,\n            args: interner.mk_args(&projection_predicate.projection_term.args.as_slice()[1..]),\n            term: projection_predicate.term,\n            use_existential_projection_new_instead: (),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialProjection<I>> {\n    pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ty::Binder<I, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Debug)]\n#[cfg_attr(feature = \"nightly\", derive(Encodable, Decodable, HashStable_NoContext))]\npub enum AliasTermKind {\n    /// A projection `<Type as Trait>::AssocType`.\n    /// Can get normalized away if monomorphic enough.\n    ProjectionTy,\n    /// An associated type in an inherent `impl`\n    InherentTy,\n    /// An opaque type (usually from `impl Trait` in type aliases or function return types)\n    /// Can only be normalized away in PostAnalysis mode or its defining scope.\n    OpaqueTy,\n    /// A free type alias that actually checks its trait bounds.\n    /// Currently only used if the type alias references opaque types.\n    /// Can always be normalized away.\n    FreeTy,\n\n    /// An unevaluated anonymous constants.\n    UnevaluatedConst,\n    /// An unevaluated const coming from an associated const.\n    ProjectionConst,\n    /// A top level const item not part of a trait or impl.\n    FreeConst,\n    /// An associated const in an inherent `impl`\n    InherentConst,\n}\n\nimpl AliasTermKind {\n    pub fn descr(self) -> &'static str {\n        match self {\n            AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => \"associated type\",\n            AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => \"associated const\",\n            AliasTermKind::InherentTy => \"inherent associated type\",\n            AliasTermKind::InherentConst => \"inherent associated const\",\n            AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => \"opaque type\",\n            AliasTermKind::FreeTy => \"type alias\",\n            AliasTermKind::FreeConst => \"unevaluated constant\",\n            AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst => \"unevaluated constant\",\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn is_type(self) -> bool {\n        match self {\n            AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\n            | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\n            | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\n            | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => true,\n\n            AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\n            | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\n            | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n            | AliasTermKind::FreeConst => false,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl From<ty::AliasTyKind> for AliasTermKind {\n    fn from(value: ty::AliasTyKind) -> Self {\n        match value {\n            ty::Projection => AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy,\n            ty::Opaque => AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy,\n            ty::Free => AliasTermKind::FreeTy,\n            ty::Inherent => AliasTermKind::InherentTy,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n/// Represents the unprojected term of a projection goal.\n///\n/// * For a projection, this would be `<Ty as Trait<...>>::N<...>`.\n/// * For an inherent projection, this would be `Ty::N<...>`.\n/// * For an opaque type, there is no explicit syntax.\n#[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\n#[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub struct AliasTerm<I: Interner> {\n    /// The parameters of the associated or opaque item.\n    ///\n    /// For a projection, these are the generic parameters for the trait and the\n    /// GAT parameters, if there are any.\n    ///\n    /// For an inherent projection, they consist of the self type and the GAT parameters,\n    /// if there are any.\n    ///\n    /// For RPIT the generic parameters are for the generics of the function,\n    /// while for TAIT it is used for the generic parameters of the alias.\n    pub args: I::GenericArgs,\n\n    /// The `DefId` of the `TraitItem` or `ImplItem` for the associated type `N` depending on whether\n    /// this is a projection or an inherent projection or the `DefId` of the `OpaqueType` item if\n    /// this is an opaque.\n    ///\n    /// During codegen, `interner.type_of(def_id)` can be used to get the type of the\n    /// underlying type if the type is an opaque.\n    ///\n    /// Note that if this is an associated type, this is not the `DefId` of the\n    /// `TraitRef` containing this associated type, which is in `interner.associated_item(def_id).container`,\n    /// aka. `interner.parent(def_id)`.\n    pub def_id: I::DefId,\n\n    /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `AliasTerm` without using [`AliasTerm::new_from_args`].\n    #[derive_where(skip(Debug))]\n    _use_alias_term_new_instead: (),\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for AliasTerm<I> {}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\n    pub fn new_from_args(interner: I, def_id: I::DefId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> AliasTerm<I> {\n        interner.debug_assert_args_compatible(def_id, args);\n        AliasTerm { def_id, args, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\n    }\n\n    pub fn new(\n        interner: I,\n        def_id: I::DefId,\n        args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\n    ) -> AliasTerm<I> {\n        let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\n        Self::new_from_args(interner, def_id, args)\n    }\n\n    pub fn expect_ty(self, interner: I) -> ty::AliasTy<I> {\n        match self.kind(interner) {\n            AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\n            | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\n            | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\n            | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => {}\n            AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n            | AliasTermKind::FreeConst\n            | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\n            | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => {\n                panic!(\"Cannot turn `UnevaluatedConst` into `AliasTy`\")\n            }\n        }\n        ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () }\n    }\n\n    pub fn kind(self, interner: I) -> AliasTermKind {\n        interner.alias_term_kind(self)\n    }\n\n    pub fn to_term(self, interner: I) -> I::Term {\n        match self.kind(interner) {\n            AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => Ty::new_alias(\n                interner,\n                ty::AliasTyKind::Projection,\n                ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n            )\n            .into(),\n            AliasTermKind::InherentTy => Ty::new_alias(\n                interner,\n                ty::AliasTyKind::Inherent,\n                ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n            )\n            .into(),\n            AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => Ty::new_alias(\n                interner,\n                ty::AliasTyKind::Opaque,\n                ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n            )\n            .into(),\n            AliasTermKind::FreeTy => Ty::new_alias(\n                interner,\n                ty::AliasTyKind::Free,\n                ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n            )\n            .into(),\n            AliasTermKind::FreeConst\n            | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n            | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\n            | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => I::Const::new_unevaluated(\n                interner,\n                ty::UnevaluatedConst::new(self.def_id.try_into().unwrap(), self.args),\n            )\n            .into(),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n/// The following methods work only with (trait) associated term projections.\nimpl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\n    pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\n        self.args.type_at(0)\n    }\n\n    pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> Self {\n        AliasTerm::new(\n            interner,\n            self.def_id,\n            [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)),\n        )\n    }\n\n    pub fn trait_def_id(self, interner: I) -> I::TraitId {\n        assert!(\n            matches!(\n                self.kind(interner),\n                AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\n            ),\n            \"expected a projection\"\n        );\n        interner.parent(self.def_id).try_into().unwrap()\n    }\n\n    /// Extracts the underlying trait reference and own args from this projection.\n    /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as StreamingIterator>::Item<'a>`,\n    /// then this function would return a `T: StreamingIterator` trait reference and\n    /// `['a]` as the own args.\n    pub fn trait_ref_and_own_args(self, interner: I) -> (TraitRef<I>, I::GenericArgsSlice) {\n        interner.trait_ref_and_own_args_for_alias(self.def_id, self.args)\n    }\n\n    /// Extracts the underlying trait reference from this projection.\n    /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as Iterator>::Item`,\n    /// then this function would return a `T: Iterator` trait reference.\n    ///\n    /// WARNING: This will drop the args for generic associated types\n    /// consider calling [Self::trait_ref_and_own_args] to get those\n    /// as well.\n    pub fn trait_ref(self, interner: I) -> TraitRef<I> {\n        self.trait_ref_and_own_args(interner).0\n    }\n\n    /// Extract the own args from this projection.\n    /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as StreamingIterator>::Item<'a>`,\n    /// then this function would return the slice `['a]` as the own args.\n    pub fn own_args(self, interner: I) -> I::GenericArgsSlice {\n        self.trait_ref_and_own_args(interner).1\n    }\n}\n\n/// The following methods work only with inherent associated term projections.\nimpl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\n    /// Transform the generic parameters to have the given `impl` args as the base and the GAT args on top of that.\n    ///\n    /// Does the following transformation:\n    ///\n    /// ```text\n    /// [Self, P_0...P_m] -> [I_0...I_n, P_0...P_m]\n    ///\n    ///     I_i impl args\n    ///     P_j GAT args\n    /// ```\n    pub fn rebase_inherent_args_onto_impl(\n        self,\n        impl_args: I::GenericArgs,\n        interner: I,\n    ) -> I::GenericArgs {\n        debug_assert!(matches!(\n            self.kind(interner),\n            AliasTermKind::InherentTy | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n        ));\n        interner.mk_args_from_iter(impl_args.iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)))\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> From<ty::AliasTy<I>> for AliasTerm<I> {\n    fn from(ty: ty::AliasTy<I>) -> Self {\n        AliasTerm { args: ty.args, def_id: ty.def_id, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> From<ty::UnevaluatedConst<I>> for AliasTerm<I> {\n    fn from(ct: the\n/// syntax, but it roughly corresponds to the syntactic forms:\n///\n/// 1. `T: TraitRef<..., Item = Type>`\n/// 2. `<T as TraitRef<...>>::Item == Type` (NYI)\n///\n/// In particular, form #1 is \"desugared\" to the combination of a\n/// normal trait predicate (`T: TraitRef<...>`) and one of these\n/// predicates. Form #2 is a broader form in that it also permits\n/// equality between arbitrary types. Ty, len: TyConst) -> Ty {\n        Ty::from_rigid_kind(RigidTy::Array(elem_ty, len))\n    }\n\n    /// Create a new pointer type.\n    pub fn new_ptr(pointee_ty: Ty, mutability: Mutability) -> Ty {\n        Ty::from_rigid_kind(RigidTy::RawPtr(pointee_ty, mutability))\n    }\n\n    /// Create a new reference type.\n    pub fn new_ref(reg: Region, pointee_ty: Ty, mutability: Mutability) -> Ty {\n        Ty::from_rigid_kind(RigidTy::Ref(reg, pointee_ty, mutability))\n    }\n\n    /// Create a new pointer type.\n    pub fn new_tuple(tys: &[Ty]) -> Ty {\n        Ty::from_rigid_kind(RigidTy::Tuple(Vec::from(tys)))\n    }\n\n    /// Create a new closure type.\n    pub fn new_closure(def: ClosureDef, args: GenericArgs) -> Ty {\n        Ty::from_rigid_kind(RigidTy::Closure(def, args))\n    }\n\n    /// Create a new coroutine type.\n    pub fn new_coroutine(def: CoroutineDef, args: GenericArgs) -> Ty {\n        Ty::from_rigid_kind(RigidTy::Coroutine(def, args))\n    }\n\n    /// Create a new closure type.\n    pub fn new_coroutine_closure(def: CoroutineClosureDef, args: GenericArgs) -> Ty {\n        Ty::from_rigid_kind(RigidTy::CoroutineClosure(def, args))\n    }\n\n    /// Create a new box type that represents `Box<T>`, for the given inner type `T`.\n    pub fn new_box(inner_ty: Ty) -> Ty {\n        with(|cx| cx.new_box_ty(inner_ty))\n    }\n\n    /// Create a type representing `usize`.\n    pub fn usize_ty() -> Ty {\n        Ty::from_rigid_kind(RigidTy::Uint(UintTy::Usize))\n    }\n\n    /// Create a type representing `bool`.\n    pub fn bool_ty() -> Ty {\n        Ty::from_rigid_kind(RigidTy::Bool)\n    }\n\n    /// Create a type representing a signed integer.\n    pub fn signed_ty(inner: IntTy) -> Ty {\n        Ty::from_rigid_kind(RigidTy::Int(inner))\n    }\n\n    /// Create a type representing an unsigned integer.\n    pub fn unsigned_ty(inner: UintTy) -> Ty {\n        Ty::from_rigid_kind(RigidTy::Uint(inner))\n    }\n\n    /// Get a type layout.\n    pub fn layout(self) -> Result<Layout, Error> {\n        with(|cx| cx.ty_layout(self))\n    }\n}\n\nimpl Ty {\n    pub fn kind(&self) -> TyKind {\n        with(|context| context.ty_kind(*self))\n    }\n}\n\n/// Represents a pattern in the type system\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Serialize)]\npub enum Pattern {\n    Range { start: Option<TyConst>, end: Option<TyConst>, include_end: bool },\n}\n\n/// Represents a constant in the type system\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Serialize)]\npub struct TyConst {\n    pub(crate) kind: TyConstKind,\n    pub id: TyConstId,\n}\n\nimpl TyConst {\n    pub fn new(kind: TyConstKind, id: TyConstId) -> TyConst {\n        Self { kind, id }\n    }\n\n    /// Retrieve the constant kind.\n    pub fn kind(&self) -> &TyConstKind {\n        &self.kind\n    }\n\n    /// Creates an interned usize constant.\n    pub fn try_from_target_usize(val: u64) -> Result<Self, Error> {\n        with(|cx| cx.try_new_ty_const_uint(val.into(), UintTy::Usize))\n    }\n\n    /// Try to evaluate to a target `usize`.\n    pub fn eval_target_usize(&self) -> Result<u64, Error> {\n        with(|cx| cx.eval_target_usize_ty(self))\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Serialize)]\npub enum TyConstKind {\n    Param(ParamConst),\n    Bound(DebruijnIndex, BoundVar),\n    Unevaluated(ConstDef, GenericArgs),\n\n    // FIXME: These should be a valtree\n    Value(Ty, Allocation),\n    ZSTValue(Ty),\n}\n\n#[derive(Copy, Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Hash)]\npub struct TyConstId(usize, ThreadLocalIndex);\n\n/// Represents a constant in MIR\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Serialize)]\npub struct MirConst {\n    /// The constant kind.\n    pub(crate) kind: ConstantKind,\n    /// The constant type.\n    pub(crate) ty: Ty,\n    /// Used for internal tracking of the internal constant.\n    pub id: MirConstId,\n}\n\nimpl MirConst {\n    /// Build a constant. Note that this should only be used by the compiler.\n    pub fn new(kind: ConstantKind, ty: Ty, id: MirConstId) -> MirConst {\n        MirConst { kind, ty, id }\n    }\n\n    /// Retrieve the constant kind.\n    pub fn kind(&self) -> &ConstantKind {\n        &self.kind\n    }\n\n    /// Get the constant type.\n    pub fn ty(&self) -> Ty {\n        self.ty\n    }\n\n    /// Try to evaluate to a target `usize`.\n    pub fn eval_target_usize(&self) -> Result<u64, Error> {\n        with(|cx| cx.eval_target_usize(self))\n    }\n\n    /// Create a constant that represents a new zero-sized constant of type T.\n    /// Fails if the type is not a ZST or if it doesn't have a known size.\n    pub fn try_new_zero_sized(ty: Ty) -> Result<MirConst, Error> {\n        with(|cx| cx.try_new_const_zst(ty))\n    }\n\n    /// Build a new constant that represents the given string.\n    ///\n    /// Note that there is no guarantee today about duplication of the same constant.\n    /// I.e.: Calling this function multiple times with the same argument may or may not return\n    /// the same allocation.\n    pub fn from_str(value: &str) -> MirConst {\n        with(|cx| cx.new_const_str(value))\n    }\n\n    /// Build a new constant that represents the given boolean value.\n    pub fn from_bool(value: bool) -> MirConst {\n        with(|cx| cx.new_const_bool(value))\n    }\n\n    /// Build a new constant that represents the given unsigned integer.\n    pub fn try_from_uint(value: u128, uint_ty: UintTy) -> Result<MirConst, Error> {\n        with(|cx| cx.try_new_const_uint(value, uint_ty))\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]\npub struct MirConstId(usize, ThreadLocalIndex);\n\ntype Ident = Opaque;\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Serialize)]\npub struct Region {\n    pub kind: RegionKind,\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Serialize)]\npub enum RegionKind {\n    ReEarlyParam(EarlyParamRegion),\n    ReBound(DebruijnIndex, BoundRegion),\n    ReStatic,\n    RePlaceholder(Placeholder<BoundRegion>),\n    ReErased,\n}\n\npub(crate) type DebruijnIndex = u32;\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Serialize)]\npub struct EarlyParamRegion {\n    pub index: u32,\n    pub name: Symbol,\n}\n\npub(crate) type BoundVar = u32;\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Serialize)]\npub struct BoundRegion {\n    pub var: BoundVar,\n    pub kind: BoundRegionKind,\n}\n\npub(crate) type UniverseIndex = u32;\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Serialize)]\npub struct Placeholder<T> {\n    pub universe: UniverseIndex,\n    pub bound: T,\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]\npub struct Span(usize, ThreadLocalIndex);\n\nimpl Debug for Span {\n    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\n        f.debug_struct(\"Span\")\n            .field(\"id\", &self.0)\n            .field(\"repr\", &with(|cx| cx.span_to_string(*self)))\n            .finish()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl Span {\n    /// Return filename for diagnostic purposes\n    pub fn get_filename(&self) -> Filename {\n        with(|c| c.get_filename(self))\n    }\n\n    /// Return lines that correspond to this `Span`\n    pub fn get_lines(&self) -> LineInfo {\n        with(|c| c.get_lines(self))\n    }\n\n    /// Return the span location to be printed in diagnostic messages.\n    ///\n    /// This may leak local file paths and should not be used to build artifacts that may be\n    /// distributed.\n    pub fn diagnostic(&self) -> String {\n        with(|c| c.span_to_string(*self))\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, Serialize)]\n/// Information you get from `Span` in a struct form.\n/// Line and col start from 1.\npub struct LineInfo {\n    pub start_line: usize,\n    pub start_col: usize,\n    pub end_line: usize,\n    pub end_col: usize,\n}\n\nimpl LineInfo {\n    pub fn from(lines: (usize, usize, usize, usize)) -> Self {\n        LineInfo { start_line: lines.0, start_col: lines.1, end_line: lines.2, end_col: lines.3 }\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Serialize)]\npub enum TyKind {\n    RigidTy(RigidTy),\n    Alias(AliasKind, AliasTy),\n    Param(ParamTy),\n    Bound(usize, BoundTy),\n}\n\nimpl TyKind {\n    pub fn rigid(&self) -> Option<&RigidTy> {\n        if let TyKind::RigidTy(inner) = self { Some(inner) } else { None }\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_unit(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Tuple(data)) if data.is_empty())\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_bool(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Bool))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_char(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Char))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_trait(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Dynamic(_, _)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_enum(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Adt(def, _)) if def.kind() == AdtKind::Enum)\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_struct(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Adt(def, _)) if def.kind() == AdtKind::Struct)\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_union(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Adt(def, _)) if def.kind() == AdtKind::Union)\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_adt(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Adt(..)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_ref(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Ref(..)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_fn(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::FnDef(..)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_fn_ptr(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::FnPtr(..)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_primitive(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(\n            self,\n            TyKind::RigidTy(\n                RigidTy::Bool\n                    | RigidTy::Char\n                    | RigidTy::Int(_)\n                    | RigidTy::Uint(_)\n                    | RigidTy::Float(_)\n            )\n        )\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_float(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Float(_)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_integral(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Int(_) | RigidTy::Uint(_)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_numeric(&self) -> bool {\n        self.is_integral() || self.is_float()\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_signed(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Int(_)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_str(&self) -> bool {\n        *self == TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Str)\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_cstr(&self) -> bool {\n        let TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Adt(def, _)) = self else {\n            return false;\n        };\n        with(|cx| cx.adt_is_cstr(*def))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_slice(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Slice(_)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_array(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Array(..)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_mutable_ptr(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(\n            self,\n            TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::RawPtr(_, Mutability::Mut))\n                | TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Ref(_, _, Mutability::Mut))\n        )\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_raw_ptr(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::RawPtr(..)))\n    }\n\n    /// Tests if this is any kind of primitive pointer type (reference, raw pointer, fn pointer).\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_any_ptr(&self) -> bool {\n        self.is_ref() || self.is_raw_ptr() || self.is_fn_ptr()\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_coroutine(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Coroutine(..)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_closure(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Closure(..)))\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_box(&self) -> bool {\n        match self {\n            TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Adt(def, _)) => def.is_box(),\n            _ => false,\n        }\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_simd(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Adt(def, _)) if def.is_simd())\n    }\n\n    pub fn trait_principal(&self) -> Option<Binder<ExistentialTraitRef>> {\n        if let TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Dynamic(predicates, _)) = self {\n            if let Some(Binder { value: ExistentialPredicate::Trait(trait_ref), bound_vars }) =\n                predicates.first()\n            {\n                Some(Binder { value: trait_ref.clone(), bound_vars: bound_vars.clone() })\n            } else {\n                None\n            }\n        } else {\n            None\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Returns the type of `ty[i]` for builtin types.\n    pub fn builtin_index(&self) -> Option<Ty> {\n        match self.rigid()? {\n            RigidTy::Array(ty, _) | RigidTy::Slice(ty) => Some(*ty),\n            _ => None,\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Returns the type and mutability of `*ty` for builtin types.\n    ///\n    /// The parameter `explicit` indicates if this is an *explicit* dereference.\n    /// Some types -- notably raw ptrs -- can only be dereferenced explicitly.\n    pub fn builtin_deref(&self, explicit: bool) -> Option<TypeAndMut> {\n        match self.rigid()? {\n            RigidTy::Adt(def, args) if def.is_box() => {\n                Some(TypeAndMut { ty: *args.0.first()?.ty()?, mutability: Mutability::Not })\n            }\n            RigidTy::Ref(_, ty, mutability) => {\n                Some(TypeAndMut { ty: *ty, mutability: *mutability })\n            }\n            RigidTy::RawPtr(ty, mutability) if explicit => {\n                Some(TypeAndMut { ty: *ty, mutability: *mutability })\n            }\n            _ => None,\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Get the function signature for function like types (Fn, FnPtr, and Closure)\n    pub fn fn_sig(&self) -> Option<PolyFnSig> {\n        match self {\n            TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::FnDef(def, args)) => Some(with(|cx| cx.fn_sig(*def, args))),\n            TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::FnPtr(sig)) => Some(sig.clone()),\n            TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Closure(_def, args)) => Some(with(|cx| cx.closure_sig(args))),\n            _ => None,\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Get the discriminant type for this type.\n    pub fn discriminant_ty(&self) -> Option<Ty> {\n        self.rigid().map(|ty| with(|cx| cx.rigid_ty_discriminant_ty(ty)))\n    }\n\n    /// Deconstruct a function type if this is one.\n    pub fn fn_def(&self) -> Option<(FnDef, &GenericArgs)> {\n        if let TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::FnDef(def, args)) = self {\n            Some((*def, args))\n        } else {\n            None\n        }\n    }\n}\n\npub struct TypeAndMut {\n    pub ty: Ty,\n    pub mutability: Mutability,\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Serialize)]\npub enum RigidTy {\n    Bool,\n    Char,\n    Int(IntTy),\n    Uint(UintTy),\n    Float(FloatTy),\n    Adt(AdtDef, GenericArgs),\n    Foreign(ForeignDef),\n    Str,\n    Array(Ty, TyConst),\n    Pat(Ty, Pattern),\n    Slice(Ty),\n    RawPtr(Ty, Mutability),\n    Ref(Region, Ty, Mutability),\n    FnDef(FnDef, GenericArgs),\n    FnPtr(PolyFnSig),\n    Closure(ClosureDef, GenericArgs),\n    Coroutine(CoroutineDef, GenericArgs),\n    CoroutineClosure(CoroutineClosureDef, GenericArgs),\n    Dynamic(Vec<Binder<ExistentialPredicate>>, Region),\n    Never,\n    Tuple(Vec<Ty>),\n    CoroutineWitness(CoroutineWitnessDef, GenericArgs),\n}\n\nimpl RigidTy {\n    /// Get the discriminant type for this type.\n    pub fn discriminant_ty(&self) -> Ty {\n        with(|cx| cx.rigid_ty_discriminant_ty(self))\n    }\n}\n\nimpl From<RigidTy> for TyKind {\n    fn from(value: RigidTy) -> Self {\n        TyKind::RigidTy(value)\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize)]\npub enum IntTy {\n    Isize,\n    I8,\n    I16,\n    I32,\n    I64,\n    I128,\n}\n\nimpl IntTy {\n    pub fn num_bytes(self) -> usize {\n        match self {\n            IntTy::Isize => MachineInfo::target_pointer_width().bytes(),\n            IntTy::I8 => 1,\n            IntTy::I16 => 2,\n            IntTy::I32 => 4,\n            IntTy::I64 => 8,\n            IntTy::I128 => 16,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize)]\npub enum UintTy {\n    Usize,\n    U8,\n    U16,\n    U32,\n    U64,\n    U128,\n}\n\nimpl UintTy {\n    pub fn num_bytes(self) -> usize {\n        match self {\n            UintTy::Usize => MachineInfo::target_pointer_width().bytes(),\n            UintTy::U8 => 1,\n            UintTy::U16 => 2,\n            UintTy::U32 => 4,\n            UintTy::U64 => 8,\n            UintTy::U128 => 16,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize)]\npub enum FloatTy {\n    F16,\n    F32,\n    F64,\n    F128,\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize)]\npub enum Movability {\n    Static,\n    Movable,\n}\n\ncrate_def! {\n    #[derive(Serialize)]\n    pub ForeignModuleDef;\n}\n\nimpl ForeignModuleDef {\n    pub fn module(&self) -> ForeignModule {\n        with(|cx| cx.foreign_module(*self))\n    }\n}\n\npub struct ForeignModule {\n    pub def_id: /// Hold information about a ForeignItem in a crate.\n    #[derive(Serialize)]\n    pub ForeignDef;\n}\n\nimpl ForeignDef {\n    pub fn kind(&self) -> ForeignItemKind {\n        with(|cx| cx.foreign_item_kind(*self))\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Debug, Hash, Serialize)]\npub enum ForeignItemKind {\n    Fn(FnDef),\n    Static(StaticDef),\n    Type(Ty),\n}\n\ncrate_def_with_ty! {\n    /// Hold information about a function definition in a crate.\n    #[derive(Serialize)]\n    pub FnDef;\n}\n\nimpl FnDef {\n    // Get the function body if available.\n    pub fn body(&self) -> Option<Body> {\n        with(|ctx| ctx.has_body(self.0).then(|| ctx.mir_body(self.0)))\n    }\n\n    // Check if the function body is available.\n    pub fn has_body(&self) -> bool {\n        with(|ctx| ctx.has_body(self.0))\n    }\n\n    /// Get the information of the intrinsic if this function is a definition of one.\n    pub fn as_intrinsic(&self) -> Option<IntrinsicDef> {\n        with(|cx| cx.intrinsic(self.def_id()))\n    }\n\n    /// Check if the function is an intrinsic.\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn is_intrinsic(&self) -> bool {\n        self.as_intrinsic().is_some()\n    }\n\n    /// Get the function signature for this function definition.\n    pub fn fn_sig(&self) -> PolyFnSig {\n        let kind = self.ty().kind();\n        kind.fn_sig().unwrap()\n    }\n}\n\ncrate_def_with_ty! {\n    #[derive(Serialize)]\n    pub IntrinsicDef;\n}\n\nimpl IntrinsicDef {\n    /// Returns the plain name of the intrinsic.\n    /// e.g., body and all backends\n    /// need to shim all calls to it.\n    pub fn must_be_overridden(&self) -> bool {\n        with(|cx| !cx.has_body(self.0))\n    }\n}\n\nimpl From<IntrinsicDef> for FnDef {\n    fn from(def: IntrinsicDef) -> Self {\n        FnDef(def.0)\n    }\n}\n\ncrate_def! {\n    #[derive(Serialize)]\n    pub ClosureDef;\n}\n\nimpl ClosureDef {\n    /// Retrieves the body of the discriminant_for_variant(&self, args: &GenericArgs, idx: VariantIdx) -> Discr {\n        with(|cx| cx.coroutine_discr_for_variant(*self, args, idx))\n    }\n}\n\ncrate_def! {\n    #[derive(Serialize)]\n    pub CoroutineClosureDef;\n}\n\ncrate_def! {\n    #[derive(Serialize)]\n    pub ParamDef;\n}\n\ncrate_def! {\n    #[derive(Serialize)]\n    pub BrNamedDef;\n}\n\ncrate_def! {\n    #[derive(Serialize)]\n    pub AdtDef;\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Debug, Hash, Serialize)]\npub enum AdtKind {\n    Enum,\n    Union,\n    Struct,\n}\n\nimpl AdtDef {\n    pub fn kind(&self) -> AdtKind {\n        with(|cx| cx.adt_kind(*self))\n    }\n\n    /// Retrieve the type of this Adt.\n    pub fn ty(&self) -> Ty {\n        with(|cx| cx.def_ty(self.0))\n    }\n\n    /// Retrieve the type of this Adt by instantiating and normalizing it with the given arguments.\n    ///\n    /// This will assume the type can be instantiated with these arguments.\n    pub fn ty_with_args(&self, args: &GenericArgs) -> Ty {\n        with(|cx| cx.def_ty_with_args(self.0, args))\n    }\n\n    pub fn is_box(&self) -> bool {\n        with(|cx| cx.adt_is_box(*self))\n    }\n\n    pub fn is_simd(&self) -> bool {\n        with(|cx| cx.adt_is_simd(*self))\n    }\n\n    /// The number of variants in this ADT.\n    pub fn num_variants(&self) -> usize {\n        with(|cx| cx.adt_variants_len(*self))\n    }\n\n    /// Retrieve the variants in this ADT.\n    pub fn variants(&self) -> Vec<VariantDef> {\n        self.variants_iter().collect()\n    }\n\n    /// Iterate over the variants in this ADT.\n    pub fn variants_iter(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = VariantDef> {\n        (0..self.num_variants())\n            .map(|idx| VariantDef { idx: VariantIdx::to_val(idx), adt_def: *self })\n    }\n\n    pub fn variant(&self, idx: VariantIdx) -> Option<VariantDef> {\n        (idx.to_index() < self.num_variants()).then_some(VariantDef { idx, adt_def: *self })\n    }\n\n    pub fn repr(&self) -> ReprOptions {\n        with(|cx| cx.adt_repr(*self))\n    }\n\n    pub fn discriminant_for_variant(&self, idx: VariantIdx) -> Discr {\n        with(|cx| cx.adt_discr_for_variant(*self, idx))\n    }\n}\n\npub struct Discr {\n    pub val: u128,\n    pub ty: Ty,\n}\n\n/// Definition of a variant, which can be either a struct / union field or an enum variant.\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Serialize)]\npub struct VariantDef {\n    /// The variant index.\n    ///\n    /// ## Warning\n    /// Do not access this field directly!\n    pub idx: VariantIdx,\n    /// The data type where this variant comes from.\n    /// For now, we use this to retrieve information about the variant itself so we don't need to\n    /// cache more       PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::Trait(TraitPredicate {\n                    trait_ref,\n                    polarity,\n                })) =>        })\n            .transpose()?;\n\n        Some(tcx.mk_predicate(kind))\n    }\n\n    /// Whether this projection can be soundly normalized.\n    ///\n    /// Wf predicates must not be normalized, as normalization\n    /// can remove required bounds which would cause us to\n    /// unsoundly accept some programs. See #91068.\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn allow_normalization(self) -> bool {\n        match self.kind().skip_binder() {\n            PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::WellFormed(_)) | PredicateKind::AliasRelate(..) => {\n                false\n            }\n            PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::Trait(_))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::HostEffect(..))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::RegionOutlives(_))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::TypeOutlives(_))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::Projection(_))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::ConstArgHasType(..))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::UnstableFeature(_))\n            | PredicateKind::DynCompatible(_)\n            | PredicateKind::Subtype(_)\n            | PredicateKind::Coerce(_)\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::ConstEvaluatable(_))\n            | PredicateKind::ConstEquate(_, _)\n            | PredicateKind::NormalizesTo(..)\n            | PredicateKind::Ambiguous => true,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_errors::IntoDiagArg for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn into_diag_arg(self, path: &mut Option<std::path::PathBuf>) -> rustc_errors::DiagArgValue {\n        ty::tls::with(|tcx| {\n            let pred = tcx.short_string(self, path);\n            rustc_errors::DiagArgValue::Str(std::borrow::Cow::Owned(pred))\n        })\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_errors::IntoDiagArg for Clause<'tcx> {\n    fn into_diag_arg(self, path: &mut Option<std::path::PathBuf>) -> rustc_errors::DiagArgValue {\n        ty::tls::with(|tcx| {\n            let clause = tcx.short_string(self, path);\n            rustc_errors::DiagArgValue::Str(std::borrow::Cow::Owned(clause))\n        })\n    }\n}\n\n/// A subset of predicates which can be assumed by the trait solver. Kind = ty::Binder<'tcx, ClauseKind<'tcx>>;\n\n    fn kind(self) -> Self::Kind {\n        self.kind()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> Clause<'tcx> {\n    pub fn as_predicate(self) -> Predicate<'tcx> {\n        Predicate(self.0)\n    }\n\n    pub fn kind(self) -> ty::Binder<'tcx, ClauseKind<'tcx>> {\n        self.0.internee.map_bound(|kind| match kind {\n            PredicateKind::Clause(clause) => clause,\n            _ => unreachable!(),\n        })\n    }\n\n    pub fn as_trait_clause(self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitPredicate<'tcx>>> {\n        let clause = self.kind();\n        if let ty::ClauseKind::Trait(trait_clause) = clause.skip_binder() {\n            Some(clause.rebind(trait_clause))\n        } else {\n            None\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn as_projection_clause(self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, ProjectionPredicate<'tcx>>> {\n        let clause = self.kind();\n        if let ty::ClauseKind::Projection(projection_clause) = clause.skip_binder() {\n            Some(clause.rebind(projection_clause))\n        } else {\n            None\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn as_type_outlives_clause(self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, TypeOutlivesPredicate<'tcx>>> {\n        let clause = self.kind();\n        if let ty::ClauseKind::TypeOutlives(o) = clause.skip_binder() {\n            Some(clause.rebind(o))\n        } else {\n            None\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn as_region_outlives_clause(\n        self,\n    ) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, RegionOutlivesPredicate<'tcx>>> {\n        let clause = self.kind();\n        if let ty::ClauseKind::RegionOutlives(o) = clause.skip_binder() {\n            Some(clause.rebind(o))\n        } else {\n            None\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::Clauses<TyCtxt<'tcx>> for ty::Clauses<'tcx> {}\n\n#[extension(pub trait ExistentialPredicateStableCmpExt<'tcx>)]\nimpl<'tcx> ExistentialPredicate<'tcx> {\n    /// Compares via an ordering that will not change if modules are reordered or other changes are\n    /// made to the tree. In particular, this ordering is preserved across incremental compilations.\n    fn stable_cmp(&self, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>, other: &Self) -> Ordering {\n        match (*self, *other) {\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => Ordering::Equal,\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref b)) => {\n                tcx.def_path_hash(a.def_id).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(b.def_id))\n            }\n            (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref b)) => {\n                tcx.def_path_hash(*a).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(*b))\n            }\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => {\n                Ordering::Greater\n            }\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\n            (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_), _) => Ordering::Greater,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\npub type PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>;\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::BoundExistentialPredicates<TyCtxt<'tcx>>\n    for &'tcx ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>\n{\n    fn principal_def_id(self) -> Option<DefId> {\n        self.principal_def_id()\n    }\n\n    fn principal(self) -> Option<ty::PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>> {\n        self.principal()\n    }\n\n    fn auto_traits(self) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = DefId> {\n        self.auto_traits()\n    }\n\n    fn projection_bounds(\n        self,\n    ) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\n        self.projection_bounds()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\n    /// Returns the \"principal `DefId`\" of this set of existential predicates.\n    ///\n    /// A Rust trait object type consists (in addition to a lifetime bound)\n    /// of a set of trait bounds, which are separated into any number\n    /// of auto-trait bounds, and at most one non-auto-trait bound. The\n    /// non-auto-trait bound is called the \"principal\" of the trait\n    /// object.\n    ///\n    /// Only the principal can have methods or type parameters (because\n    /// auto traits can have neither of them). This is important, because\n    /// it means the auto traits can be treated as an unordered set (methods\n    /// would force an order for the vtable, while relating traits with\n    /// type parameters without knowing the order to relate them in is\n    /// a rather non-trivial task).\n    ///\n    /// For example, in the trait object `dyn std::fmt::Debug + Sync`, the\n    /// principal bound is `Some(std::fmt::Debug)`, while the auto-trait bounds\n    /// are the set `{Sync}`.\n    ///\n    /// It is also possible to have a \"trivial\" trait object that\n    /// consists only of auto traits, with no principal - for example,\n    /// `dyn Send + Sync`. In that case, the set of auto-trait bounds\n    /// is `{Send, Sync}`, while there is no principal. These trait objects\n    /// have a \"trivial\" vtable consisting of just the size, alignment,\n    /// and destructor.\n    pub fn principal(&self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>> {\n        self[0]\n            .map_bound(|this| match this {\n                ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => Some(tr),\n                _ => None,\n            })\n            .transpose()\n    }\n\n    pub fn principal_def_id(&self) -> Option<DefId> {\n        self.principal().map(|trait_ref| trait_ref.skip_binder().def_id)\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn projection_bounds(\n        &self,\n    ) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\n        self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| {\n            predicate\n                .map_bound(|pred| match pred {\n                    ExistentialPredicate::Projection(projection) => Some(projection),\n                    _ => None,\n                })\n                .transpose()\n        })\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = DefId> {\n        self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| match predicate.skip_binder() {\n            ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(did) => Some(did),\n            _ => None,\n        })\n    }\n\n    pub fn without_auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\n        self.iter().filter(|predicate| {\n            !matches!(predicate.as_ref().skip_binder(), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_))\n        })\n    }\n}\n\npub type PolyTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitRef<'tcx>>;\npub type PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>;\npub type PolyExistentialProjection<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>;\n\nimpl<'tcx> Clause<'tcx> {\n    /// Performs a instantiation suitable for going from a\n    /// poly-trait-ref to supertraits that must hold if that\n    /// poly-trait-ref holds. This is slightly different from a normal\n    /// instantiation in terms of what happens with bound regions. See\n    /// lengthy comment below for details.\n    pub fn instantiate_supertrait(\n        self,\n        tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>,\n        trait_ref: ty::PolyTraitRef<'tcx>,\n    ) -> Clause<'tcx> {\n        // The interaction between HRTB and supertraits is not entirely\n        // obvious. Let me walk you (and myself) through an example.\n        //\n        // Let's start with an easy case. Consider two traits:\n        //\n        //     trait Foo<'a>: Bar<'a,'a> { }\n        //     trait Bar<'b,'c> { }\n        //\n        // Now, if we have a trait reference `for<'x> T: Foo<'x>`, then\n        // we can deduce that `for<'x> T: Bar<'x,'x>`. Basically, if we\n        // knew that `Foo<'x>` (for any 'x) then we also know that\n        // `Bar<'x,'x>` (for any 'x). This more-or-less falls out from\n        // normal instantiation.\n        //\n        // In terms of why this is sound, the idea is that whenever there\n        // is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>`, it must show that `T:Bar<'a,'a>`\n        // holds. So if there is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>` that applies to\n        // all `'a`, then we must know that `T:Bar<'a,'a>` holds for all\n        // `'a`.\n        //\n        // Another example to be careful of is this:\n        //\n        //     trait Foo1<'a>: for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b> { }\n        //     trait Bar1<'b,'c> { }\n        //\n        // Here, if we have `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`, then what do we know?\n        // The answer is that we know `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`. The\n        // reason is similar to the previous example: any impl of\n        // `T:Foo1<'x>` must show that `for<'b> T: Bar1<'x, 'b>`. So\n        // basically we would want to collapse the bound lifetimes from\n        // the input (`trait_ref`) and the supertraits.\n        //\n        // To achieve this in practice is fairly straightforward. Let's\n        // consider the more complicated scenario:\n        //\n        // - We start out with `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`. In this case, `'x`\n        //   has a De Bruijn index of 1. We want to produce `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`,\n        //   where both `'x` and `'b` would have a DB index of 1.\n        //   The instantiation from the input trait-ref is therefore going to be\n        //   `'a => 'x` (where `'x` has a DB index of 1).\n        // - The supertrait-ref is `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, where `'a` is an\n        //   early-bound parameter and `'b` is a late-bound parameter with a\n        //   DB index of 1.\n        // - If we replace `'a` with `'x` from the input, it too will have\n        //   a DB index of 1, and thus we'll have `for<'x,'b> Bar1<'x,'b>`\n        //   just as we wanted.\n        //\n        // There is only one catch. If we just apply the instantiation `'a\n        // => 'x` to `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, the instantiation code will\n        // adjust the DB index because we instantiating into a binder (it\n        // tries to be so smart...) resulting in `for<'x> for<'b>\n        // Bar1<'x,'b>` (we have no syntax for this, so use your\n        // imagination). Basically the 'x will have DB index of 2 and 'b\n        // will have DB index of 1. Not quite what we want. So we apply\n        // the instantiation to the *contents* of the trait reference,\n        // rather than the trait reference itself (put another way, the\n        // instantiation code expects equal binding levels in the values\n        // from the instantiation and the value being instantiated into, and\n        // this trick achieves that).\n\n        // Working through the second example:\n        // trait_ref: for<'x> T: Foo1<'^0.0>; args: [T, '^0.0]\n        // predicate: for<'b> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0>; args: [Self, 'a, '^0.0]\n        // We want to end up with:\n        //     for<'x, 'b> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\n        // To do this:\n        // 1) We must shift all bound vars in predicate by the length\n        //    of trait ref's bound vars. So, we would end up with predicate like\n        //    Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\n        // 2) We can then apply the trait args to this, ending up with\n        //    T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\n        // 3) Finally, to create the final bound vars, we concatenate the bound\n        //    vars of the trait ref with those of the predicate:\n        //    ['x, 'b]\n        let bound_pred = self.kind();\n        let pred_bound_vars = bound_pred.bound_vars();\n        let trait_bound_vars = trait_ref.bound_vars();\n        // 1) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0> -> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\n        let shifted_pred =\n            tcx.shift_bound_var_indices(trait_bound_vars.len(), bound_pred.skip_binder());\n        // 2) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1> -> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\n        let new = EarlyBinder::bind(shifted_pred).instantiate(tcx, trait_ref.skip_binder().args);\n        // 3) ['x] + ['b] -> ['x, 'b]\n        let bound_vars =\n            tcx.mk_bound_variable_kinds_from_iter(trait_bound_vars.iter().chain(pred_bound_vars));\n\n        // FIXME: Is it really perf sensitive to use reuse_or_mk_predicate here?\n        tcx.reuse_or_mk_predicate(\n            self.as_predicate(),\n            ty::Binder::bind_with_vars(PredicateKind::Clause(new), bound_vars),\n        )\n        .expect_clause()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, PredicateKind<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: PredicateKind<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        ty::Binder::dummy(from).upcast(tcx)\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        tcx.mk_predicate(from)\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ClauseKind<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: ClauseKind<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        tcx.mk_predicate(ty::Binder::dummy(PredicateKind::Clause(from)))\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ty::Binder<'tcx, ClauseKind<'tcx>>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<'tcx, ClauseKind<'tcx>>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        tcx.mk_predicate(from.map_bound(PredicateKind::Clause))\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, Clause<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: Clause<'tcx>, _tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        from.as_predicate()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ClauseKind<'tcx>> for Clause<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: ClauseKind<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        tcx.mk_predicate(ty::Binder::dummy(PredicateKind::Clause(from))).expect_clause()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ty::Binder<'tcx, ClauseKind<'tcx>>> for Clause<'tcx> {\n    fn TypeOutlivesPredicate<'tcx>>;\npub type PolySubtypePredicate<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, SubtypePredicate<'tcx>>;\npub type PolyCoercePredicate<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, CoercePredicate<'tcx>>;\npub type PolyProjectionPredicate<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ProjectionPredicate<'tcx>>;\n\n/// A statement that can be proven by a trait solver. This includes things that may\n/// show up in where clauses, such as trait predicates and projection predicates,\n/// and also things that are emitted as part of type checking such as `DynCompatible`\n/// predicate which is emitted when a type is coerced to a trait object.\n///\n/// Use this rather than `PredicateKind`, whenever possible.\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, HashStable)]\n#[rustc_pass_by_value]\npub struct Predicate<'tcx>(\n    pub(super) Interned<'tcx, WithCachedTypeInfo<ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>>>,\n);\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::Predicate<TyCtxt<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn as_clause(self) -> Option<ty::Clause<'tcx>> {\n        self.as_clause()\n    }\n\n    fn allow_normalization(self) -> bool {\n        self.allow_normalization()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::IntoKind for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    type Kind = ty::Binder<'tcx, ty::PredicateKind<'tcx>>;\n\n    fn kind(self) -> Self::Kind {\n        self.kind()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::Flags for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn flags(&self) -> TypeFlags {\n        self.0.flags\n    }\n\n    fn outer_exclusive_binder(&self) -> ty::DebruijnIndex {\n        self.0.outer_exclusive_binder\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> Predicate<'tcx> {\n    /// Gets the inner `ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>`.\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn kind(self) -> ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>> {\n        self.0.internee\n    }\n\n    // FIXME(compiler-errors): Think about removing this.\n    #[inline(always)]\n    pub fn flags(self) -> TypeFlags {\n        self.0.flags\n    }\n\n    // FIXME(compiler-errors): Think about removing this.\n    #[inline(always)]\n    pub fn outer_exclusive_binder(self) -> DebruijnIndex {\n        self.0.outer_exclusive_binder\n    }\n\n    /// Flips the polarity of a Predicate.\n    ///\n    /// Given `T: Trait` predicate it returns `T: !Trait` and given `T: !Trait` returns `T: Trait`.\n    pub fn flip_polarity(self, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Option<Predicate<'tcx>> {\n        let kind = self\n            .kind()\n            .map_bound(|kind| match kind {\n                PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::Trait(TraitPredicate {\n                    trait_ref,\n                    polarity,\n                })) => Some(PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::Trait(TraitPredicate {\n                    trait_ref,\n                    polarity: polarity.flip(),\n                }))),\n\n                _ => None,\n            })\n            .transpose()?;\n\n        Some(tcx.mk_predicate(kind))\n    }\n\n    /// Whether this projection can be soundly normalized.\n    ///\n    /// Wf predicates must not be normalized, as normalization\n    /// can remove required bounds which would cause us to\n    /// unsoundly accept some programs. See #91068.\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn allow_normalization(self) -> bool {\n        match self.kind().skip_binder() {\n            PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::WellFormed(_)) | PredicateKind::AliasRelate(..) => {\n                false\n            }\n            PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::Trait(_))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::HostEffect(..))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::RegionOutlives(_))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::TypeOutlives(_))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::Projection(_))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::ConstArgHasType(..))\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::UnstableFeature(_))\n            | PredicateKind::DynCompatible(_)\n            | PredicateKind::Subtype(_)\n            | PredicateKind::Coerce(_)\n            | PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::ConstEvaluatable(_))\n            | PredicateKind::ConstEquate(_, _)\n            | PredicateKind::NormalizesTo(..)\n            | PredicateKind::Ambiguous => true,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_errors::IntoDiagArg for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn into_diag_arg(self, path: &mut Option<std::path::PathBuf>) -> rustc_errors::DiagArgValue {\n        ty::tls::with(|tcx| {\n            let pred = tcx.short_string(self, path);\n            rustc_errors::DiagArgValue::Str(std::borrow::Cow::Owned(pred))\n        })\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_errors::IntoDiagArg for Clause<'tcx> {\n    fn into_diag_arg(self, path: &mut Option<std::path::PathBuf>) -> rustc_errors::DiagArgValue {\n        ty::tls::with(|tcx| {\n            let clause = tcx.short_string(self, path);\n            rustc_errors::DiagArgValue::Str(std::borrow::Cow::Owned(clause))\n        })\n    }\n}\n\n/// A subset of predicates which can be assumed by the trait solver. They show up in\n/// an item's where clauses, hence the name `Clause`, and may either be user-written\n/// (such as traits) or may be inserted during lowering.\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, HashStable)]\n#[rustc_pass_by_value]\npub struct Clause<'tcx>(\n    pub(super) Interned<'tcx, WithCachedTypeInfo<ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>>>,\n);\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::Clause<TyCtxt<'tcx>> for Clause<'tcx> {\n    fn as_predicate(self) -> Predicate<'tcx> {\n        self.as_predicate()\n    }\n\n    fn instantiate_supertrait(self, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>, trait_ref: ty::PolyTraitRef<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        self.instantiate_supertrait(tcx, trait_ref)\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::IntoKind for Clause<'tcx> {\n    type Kind = ty::Binder<'tcx, ClauseKind<'tcx>>;\n\n    fn kind(self) -> Self::Kind {\n        self.kind()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> Clause<'tcx> {\n    pub fn as_predicate(self) -> Predicate<'tcx> {\n        Predicate(self.0)\n    }\n\n    pub fn kind(self) -> ty::Binder<'tcx, ClauseKind<'tcx>> {\n        self.0.internee.map_bound(|kind| match kind {\n            PredicateKind::Clause(clause) => clause,\n            _ => unreachable!(),\n        })\n    }\n\n    pub fn as_trait_clause(self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitPredicate<'tcx>>> {\n        let clause = self.kind();\n        if let ty::ClauseKind::Trait(trait_clause) = clause.skip_binder() {\n            Some(clause.rebind(trait_clause))\n        } else {\n            None\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn as_projection_clause(self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, ProjectionPredicate<'tcx>>> {\n        let clause = self.kind();\n        if let ty::ClauseKind::Projection(projection_clause) = clause.skip_binder() {\n            Some(clause.rebind(projection_clause))\n        } else {\n            None\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn as_type_outlives_clause(self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, TypeOutlivesPredicate<'tcx>>> {\n        let clause = self.kind();\n        if let ty::ClauseKind::TypeOutlives(o) = clause.skip_binder() {\n            Some(clause.rebind(o))\n        } else {\n            None\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn as_region_outlives_clause(\n        self,\n    ) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, RegionOutlivesPredicate<'tcx>>> {\n        let clause = self.kind();\n        if let ty::ClauseKind::RegionOutlives(o) = clause.skip_binder() {\n            Some(clause.rebind(o))\n        } else {\n            None\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::Clauses<TyCtxt<'tcx>> for ty::Clauses<'tcx> {}\n\n#[extension(pub trait ExistentialPredicateStableCmpExt<'tcx>)]\nimpl<'tcx> ExistentialPredicate<'tcx> {\n    /// Compares via an ordering that will not change if modules are reordered or other changes are\n    /// made to the tree. In particular, this ordering is preserved across incremental compilations.\n    fn stable_cmp(&self, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>, other: &Self) -> Ordering {\n        match (*self, *other) {\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => Ordering::Equal,\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref b)) => {\n                tcx.def_path_hash(a.def_id).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(b.def_id))\n            }\n            (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref b)) => {\n                tcx.def_path_hash(*a).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(*b))\n            }\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => {\n                Ordering::Greater\n            }\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\n            (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_), _) => Ordering::Greater,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\npub type PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>;\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::BoundExistentialPredicates<TyCtxt<'tcx>>\n    for &'tcx ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>\n{\n    fn principal_def_id(self) -> Option<DefId> {\n        self.principal_def_id()\n    }\n\n    fn principal(self) -> Option<ty::PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>> {\n        self.principal()\n    }\n\n    fn auto_traits(self) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = DefId> {\n        self.auto_traits()\n    }\n\n    fn projection_bounds(\n        self,\n    ) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\n        self.projection_bounds()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\n    /// Returns the \"principal `DefId`\" of this set of existential predicates.\n    ///\n    /// A Rust trait object type consists (in addition to a lifetime bound)\n    /// of a set of trait bounds, which are separated into any number\n    /// of auto-trait bounds, and at most one non-auto-trait bound. The\n    /// non-auto-trait bound is called the \"principal\" of the trait\n    /// object.\n    ///\n    /// Only the principal can have methods or type parameters (because\n    /// auto traits can have neither of them). This is important, because\n    /// it means the auto traits can be treated as an unordered set (methods\n    /// would force an order for the vtable, while relating traits with\n    /// type parameters without knowing the order to relate them in is\n    /// a rather non-trivial task).\n    ///\n    /// For example, in the trait object `dyn std::fmt::Debug + Sync`, the\n    /// principal bound is `Some(std::fmt::Debug)`, while the auto-trait bounds\n    /// are the set `{Sync}`.\n    ///\n    /// It is also possible to have a \"trivial\" trait object that\n    /// consists only of auto traits, with no principal - for example,\n    /// `dyn Send + Sync`. In that case, the set of auto-trait bounds\n    /// is `{Send, Sync}`, while there is no principal. These trait objects\n    /// have a \"trivial\" vtable consisting of just the size, alignment,\n    /// and destructor.\n    pub fn principal(&self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>> {\n        self[0]\n            .map_bound(|this| match this {\n                ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => Some(tr),\n                _ => None,\n            })\n            .transpose()\n    }\n\n    pub fn principal_def_id(&self) -> Option<DefId> {\n        self.principal().map(|trait_ref| trait_ref.skip_binder().def_id)\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn projection_bounds(\n        &self,\n    ) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\n        self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| {\n            predicate\n                .map_bound(|pred| match pred {\n                    ExistentialPredicate::Projection(projection) => Some(projection),\n                    _ => None,\n                })\n                .transpose()\n        })\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = DefId> {\n        self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| match predicate.skip_binder() {\n            ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(did) => Some(did),\n            _ => None,\n        })\n    }\n\n    pub fn without_auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\n        self.iter().filter(|predicate| {\n            !matches!(predicate.as_ref().skip_binder(), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_))\n        })\n    }\n}\n\npub type PolyTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitRef<'tcx>>;\npub type PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>;\npub type PolyExistentialProjection<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>;\n\nimpl<'tcx> Clause<'tcx> {\n    /// Performs a instantiation suitable for going from a\n    /// poly-trait-ref to supertraits that must hold if that\n    /// poly-trait-ref holds. This is slightly different from a normal\n    /// instantiation in terms of what happens with bound regions. See\n    /// lengthy comment below for details.\n    pub fn instantiate_supertrait(\n        self,\n        tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>,\n        trait_ref: ty::PolyTraitRef<'tcx>,\n    ) -> Clause<'tcx> {\n        // The interaction between HRTB and supertraits is not entirely\n        // obvious. Let me walk you (and myself) through an example.\n        //\n        // Let's start with an easy case. Consider two traits:\n        //\n        //     trait Foo<'a>: Bar<'a,'a> { }\n        //     trait Bar<'b,'c> { }\n        //\n        // Now, if we have a trait reference `for<'x> T: Foo<'x>`, then\n        // we can deduce that `for<'x> T: Bar<'x,'x>`. Basically, if we\n        // knew that `Foo<'x>` (for any 'x) then we also know that\n        // `Bar<'x,'x>` (for any 'x). This more-or-less falls out from\n        // normal instantiation.\n        //\n        // In terms of why this is sound, the idea is that whenever there\n        // is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>`, it must show that `T:Bar<'a,'a>`\n        // holds. So if there is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>` that applies to\n        // all `'a`, then we must know that `T:Bar<'a,'a>` holds for all\n        // `'a`.\n        //\n        // Another example to be careful of is this:\n        //\n        //     trait Foo1<'a>: for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b> { }\n        //     trait Bar1<'b,'c> { }\n        //\n        // Here, if we have `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`, then what do we know?\n        // The answer is that we know `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`. The\n        // reason is similar to the previous example: any impl of\n        // `T:Foo1<'x>` must show that `for<'b> T: Bar1<'x, 'b>`. So\n        // basically we would want to collapse the bound lifetimes from\n        // the input (`trait_ref`) and the supertraits.\n        //\n        // To achieve this in practice is fairly straightforward. Let's\n        // consider the more complicated scenario:\n        //\n        // - We start out with `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`. In this case, `'x`\n        //   has a De Bruijn index of 1. We want to produce `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`,\n        //   where both `'x` and `'b` would have a DB index of 1.\n        //   The instantiation from the input trait-ref is therefore going to be\n        //   `'a => 'x` (where `'x` has a DB index of 1).\n        // - The supertrait-ref is `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, where `'a` is an\n        //   early-bound parameter and `'b` is a late-bound parameter with a\n        //   DB index of 1.\n        // - If we replace `'a` with `'x` from the input, it too will have\n        //   a DB index of 1, and thus we'll have `for<'x,'b> Bar1<'x,'b>`\n        //   just as we wanted.\n        //\n        // There is only one catch. If we just apply the instantiation `'a\n        // => 'x` to `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, the instantiation code will\n        // adjust the DB index because we instantiating into a binder (it\n        // tries to be so smart...) resulting in `for<'x> for<'b>\n        // Bar1<'x,'b>` (we have no syntax for this, so use your\n        // imagination). Basically the 'x will have DB index of 2 and 'b\n        // will have DB index of 1. Not quite what we want. So we apply\n        // the instantiation to the *contents* of the trait reference,\n        // rather than the trait reference itself (put another way, the\n        // instantiation code expects equal binding levels in the values\n        // from the instantiation and the value being instantiated into, and\n        // this trick achieves that).\n\n        // Working through the second example:\n        // trait_ref: for<'x> T: Foo1<'^0.0>; args: [T, '^0.0]\n        // predicate: for<'b> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0>; args: [Self, 'a, '^0.0]\n        // We want to end up with:\n        //     for<'x, 'b> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\n        // To do this:\n        // 1) We must shift all bound vars in predicate by the length\n        //    of trait ref's bound vars. So, we would end up with predicate like\n        //    Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\n        // 2) We can then apply the trait args to this, ending up with\n        //    T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\n        // 3) Finally, to create the final bound vars, we concatenate the bound\n        //    vars of the trait ref with those of the predicate:\n        //    ['x, 'b]\n        let bound_pred = self.kind();\n        let pred_bound_vars = bound_pred.bound_vars();\n        let trait_bound_vars = trait_ref.bound_vars();\n        // 1) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0> -> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\n        let shifted_pred =\n            tcx.shift_bound_var_indices(trait_bound_vars.len(), bound_pred.skip_binder());\n        // 2) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1> -> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\n        let new = EarlyBinder::bind(shifted_pred).instantiate(tcx, trait_ref.skip_binder().args);\n        // 3) ['x] + ['b] -> ['x, 'b]\n        let bound_vars =\n            tcx.mk_bound_variable_kinds_from_iter(trait_bound_vars.iter().chain(pred_bound_vars));\n\n        // FIXME: Is it really perf sensitive to use reuse_or_mk_predicate here?\n        tcx.reuse_or_mk_predicate(\n            self.as_predicate(),\n            ty::Binder::bind_with_vars(PredicateKind::Clause(new), bound_vars),\n        )\n        .expect_clause()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, PredicateKind<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: PredicateKind<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        ty::Binder::dummy(from).upcast(tcx)\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        tcx.mk_predicate(from)\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ClauseKind<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: ClauseKind<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        tcx.mk_predicate(ty::Binder::dummy(PredicateKind::Clause(from)))\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ty::Binder<'tcx, ClauseKind<'tcx>>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<'tcx, ClauseKind<'tcx>>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        tcx.mk_predicate(from.map_bound(PredicateKind::Clause))\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, Clause<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: Clause<'tcx>, _tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        from.as_predicate()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ClauseKind<'tcx>> for Clause<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: ClauseKind<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        tcx.mk_predicate(ty::Binder::dummy(PredicateKind::Clause(from))).expect_clause()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ty::Binder<'tcx, ClauseKind<'tcx>>> for Clause<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<'tcx, ClauseKind<'tcx>>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        tcx.mk_predicate(from.map_bound(|clause| PredicateKind::Clause(clause))).expect_clause()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, TraitRef<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: TraitRef<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        ty::Binder::dummy(from).upcast(tcx)\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, TraitRef<'tcx>> for Clause<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: TraitRef<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        let p: Predicate<'tcx> = from.upcast(tcx);\n        p.expect_clause()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitRef<'tcx>>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitRef<'tcx>>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        let pred: PolyTraitPredicate<'tcx> = from.upcast(tcx);\n        pred.upcast(tcx)\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitRef<'tcx>>> for Clause<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitRef<'tcx>>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        let pred: PolyTraitPredicate<'tcx> = from.upcast(tcx);\n        pred.upcast(tcx)\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, TraitPredicate<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: TraitPredicate<'tcx>, tcx:    p.expect_clause()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, PolyTraitPredicate<'tcx>> for Clause<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: PolyTraitPredicate<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n        let p: Predicate<'tcx> = from.upcast(tcx);\n        p.expect_clause()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, RegionOutlivesPredicate<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn in the given crate-local module.\n    Restricted(Id),\n}\n\nimpl Visibility {\n    pub fn to_string(self, def_id: LocalDefId, tcx: TyCtxt<'_>) -> String {\n        match self {\n            ty::Visibility::Restricted(restricted_id) => {\n                if restricted_id.is_top_level_module() {\n                    \"pub(crate)\".to_string()\n                } else if restricted_id == tcx.parent_module_from_def_id(def_id).to_local_def_id() {\n                    \"pub(self)\".to_string()\n                } else {\n                    format!(\n                        \"pub(in crate{})\",\n                        tcx.def_path(restricted_id.to_def_id()).to_string_no_crate_verbose()\n                    )\n                }\n            }\n            ty::Visibility::Public => \"pub\".to_string(),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Copy, Hash, TyEncodable, TyDecodable, HashStable)]\n#[derive(TypeFoldable, TypeVisitable)]\npub struct ClosureSizeProfileData<'tcx> {\n    /// Tuple containing the types of closure captures before the feature `capture_disjoint_fields`\n    pub before_feature_tys: Ty<'tcx>,\n    /// Tuple containing the types of closure captures after the feature `capture_disjoint_fields`\n    pub after_feature_tys: Ty<'tcx>,\n}\n\nimpl TyCtxt<'_> {\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn opt_parent(self, id: DefId) -> Option<DefId> {\n        self.def_key(id).parent.map(|index| DefId { index, ..id })\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    #[track_caller]\n    pub fn parent(self, id: DefId) -> DefId {\n        match self.opt_parent(id) {\n            Some(id) => id,\n            // not `unwrap_or_else` to avoid breaking caller tracking\n            None => bug!(\"{id:?} doesn't have a parent\"),\n        }\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    #[track_caller]\n    pub fn opt_local_parent(self, id: LocalDefId) -> Option<LocalDefId> {\n        self.opt_parent(id.to_def_id()).map(DefId::expect_local)\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    #[track_caller]\n    pub fn local_parent(self, id: impl Into<LocalDefId>) -> LocalDefId {\n        self.parent(id.into().to_def_id()).expect_local()\n    }\n\n    pub fn is_descendant_of(self, mut descendant: DefId, ancestor: DefId) -> bool {\n        if descendant.krate != ancestor.krate {\n            return false;\n        }\n\n        while descendant != ancestor {\n            match self.opt_parent(descendant) {\n                Some(parent) => descendant = parent,\n                None => return false,\n            }\n        }\n        true\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<Id> Visibility<Id> {\n    pub fn is_public(self) -> bool {\n        matches!(self, Visibility::Public)\n    }\n\n    pub fn map_id<OutId>(self, f: impl FnOnce(Id) -> OutId) -> Visibility<OutId> {\n        match self {\n            Visibility::Public => Visibility::Public,\n            Visibility::Restricted(id) => Visibility::Restricted(f(id)),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<Id: Into<DefId>> Visibility<Id> {\n    pub fn to_def_id(self) -> Visibility<DefId> {\n        self.map_id(Into::into)\n    }\n\n    /// Returns `true` if an item with this visibility is accessible from the given module.\n    pub fn is_accessible_from(self, module: impl Into<DefId>, tcx: TyCtxt<'_>) -> bool {\n        match self {\n            // Public items are visible everywhere.\n            Visibility::Public => true,\n            Visibility::Restricted(id) => tcx.is_descendant_of(module.into(), id.into()),\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Returns `true` if this visibility is at least as accessible as the given visibility\n    pub fn is_at_least(self, vis: Visibility<impl Into<DefId>>, tcx: TyCtxt<'_>) -> bool {\n        match vis {\n            Visibility::Public => self.is_public(),\n            Visibility::Restricted(id) => self.is_accessible_from(id, tcx),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl Visibility<DefId> {\n    pub fn expect_local(self) -> Visibility {\n        self.map_id(|id| id.expect_local())\n    }\n\n    /// Returns `true` if this item is visible anywhere in the local crate.\n    pub fn is_visible_locally(self) -> bool {\n        match self {\n            Visibility::Public => true,\n            Visibility::Restricted(def_id) => def_id.is_local(),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n/// The crate variances map is computed during typeck and contains the\n/// variance of every item in the local crate. You should not use it\n/// directly, because to do so will make your pass dependent on the\n/// HIR of every item in the local crate. Instead, use\n/// `tcx.variances_of()` to get the variance for a *particular*\n/// item.\n#[derive(HashStable, Debug)]\npub struct CrateVariancesMap<'tcx> {\n    /// For each item with generics, maps to a vector of the variance\n    /// of its generics. If an item has no generics, it will have no\n    /// entry.\n    pub variances: DefIdMap<&'tcx [ty::Variance]>,\n}\n\n// Contains information needed to resolve types and (in the future) look up\n// the types of AST nodes.\n#[derive(Copy, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]\npub struct CReaderCacheKey {\n    pub cnum: Option<CrateNum>,\n    pub pos: usize,\n}\n\n/// Use this rather than `TyKind`, whenever possible.\n#[derive(Copy, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, HashStable)]\n#[rustc_diagnostic_item = \"Ty\"]\n#[rustc_pass_by_value]\npub struct Ty<'tcx>(Interned<'tcx, WithCachedTypeInfo<TyKind<'tcx>>>);\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::IntoKind for Ty<'tcx> {\n    type Kind = TyKind<'tcx>;\n\n    fn kind(self) -> TyKind<'tcx> {\n        *self.kind()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::Flags for Ty<'tcx> {\n    fn flags(&self) -> TypeFlags {\n        self.0.flags\n    }\n\n    fn outer_exclusive_binder(&self) -> DebruijnIndex {\n        self.0.outer_exclusive_binder\n    }\n}\n\n/// The crate outlives map is computed during typeck and contains the\n/// outlives of every item in the local crate. struct Term<'tcx> {\n    ptr: NonNull<()>,\n    marker: PhantomData<(Ty<'tcx>, Const<'tcx>)>,\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::Term<TyCtxt<'tcx>> for Term<'tcx> {}\n\nimpl<'tcx>
